item.title: PANEL: A photovoltaic-specific vision-language model for zero-shot and few-shot PV interpretation tasks in remote sensing
item.dc:creator: Ruizhe Deng
Zhiling Guo
Penglei Zhang
Jiaze Li
Xin Xu
Qi Chen
Yuntian Chen
Jinyue Yan
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Large-scale photovoltaic (PV) mapping from remote sensing (RS) is crucial for solar energy assessment and infrastructure development. While vision-language models (VLMs) have advanced language-guided image understanding, existing RS CLIP models underperform on PV across changing regions, sensors, and imaging conditions due to the scarcity of PV imagery and aligned prompts in open RS multi-modal datasets. To address this, we curate the first worldwide PV vision-language dataset, comprising over one million image-text pairs spanning 0.1-20 m resolutions, and design aligned flexible binary prompts that explicitly distinguish PV-present from PV-absent imagery. We then build PANEL ( P V-specific vision-l AN guage mod EL ) by retraining CLIP using a tailored pipeline featuring extensive augmentation and prompt diversification. For zero-shot inference, we introduce PANEL Surgery to reinforce vision-language alignment and extract PV-relevant priors for PV interpretation tasks. For few-shot adaptation, we propose a plug-in Knowledge Assistance Module (KAM) that injects these priors into diverse baselines via gated convolution and cross-attention fusion. To enable rigorous evaluation, we establish seven PV-specific benchmarks spanning five interpretation tasks and observe that PANEL consistently surpasses representative CLIP models in zero-shot classification (>40% accuracy), semantic localization (>45% mSC), segmentation (>25% IoU), detection (>24% recall), and image generation (>7% Δ C L I P " role="presentation"> Δ C L I P Δ C L I P reduction). In few-shot settings, KAM generalizes across multiple architectures with minimal labels, and ablation studies confirm the necessity of vision-language priors and the complementary design of each component. We further introduce a label-efficient sampling strategy leveraging VLM-derived similarity scores, consistently outperforming random selection under tight annotation budgets. Collectively, these results establish PANEL as a unified, PV-specific VLM for robust zero-/few-shot interpretation while reducing annotation costs in real-world RS for solar energy. PANEL and benchmarks are publicly available at https://huggingface.co/UEXdo . Published: 2026-05-17T05:53:26+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.823 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruizhe Deng; Zhiling Guo; Penglei Zhang; Jiaze Li; Xin Xu; Qi Chen; Yuntian Chen; Jinyue Yan

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.012

Relevance: 0.823 (must_read)

Large-scale photovoltaic (PV) mapping from remote sensing (RS) is crucial for solar energy assessment and infrastructure development. While vision-language models (VLMs) have advanced language-guided image understanding, existing RS CLIP models underperform on PV across changing regions, sensors, and imaging conditions due to the scarcity of PV imagery and aligned prompts in open RS multi-modal datasets. To address this, we curate the first worldwide PV vision-language dataset, comprising over one million image-text pairs spanning 0.1-20 m resolutions, and design aligned flexible binary prompts that explicitly distinguish PV-present from PV-absent imagery. We then build PANEL ( P V-specific vision-l AN guage mod EL ) by retraining CLIP using a tailored pipeline featuring extensive augmentation and prompt diversification. For zero-shot inference, we introduce PANEL Surgery to reinforce vision-language alignment and extract PV-relevant priors for PV interpretation tasks. For few-shot adaptation, we propose a plug-in Knowledge Assistance Module (KAM) that injects these priors into diverse baselines via gated convolution and cross-attention fusion. To enable rigorous evaluation, we establish seven PV-specific benchmarks spanning five interpretation tasks and observe that PANEL consistently surpasses representative CLIP models in zero-shot classification (>40% accuracy), semantic localization (>45% mSC), segmentation (>25% IoU), detection (>24% recall), and image generation (>7% Δ C L I P " role="presentation"> Δ C L I P Δ C L I P reduction). In few-shot settings, KAM generalizes across multiple architectures with minimal labels, and ablation studies confirm the necessity of vision-language priors and the complementary design of each component. We further introduce a label-efficient sampling strategy leveraging VLM-derived similarity scores, consistently outperforming random selection under tight annotation budgets. Collectively, these results establish PANEL as a unified, PV-specific VLM for robust zero-/few-shot interpretation while reducing annotation costs in real-world RS for solar energy. PANEL and benchmarks are publicly available at https://huggingface.co/UEXdo .

item.title: Intelligent Cover-Pulse Design for Cognitive Radar Anti-Jamming via Reinforcement Learning
item.dc:creator: Shanhong Guo
Junxuan Li
Weihao Shi
Bi Wen
Xiaoyu Cong
Weixing Sheng
item.description: Traditional frequency-agile radars can no longer effectively counter cognitive jammers, and conventional cover-pulse techniques fail to address their dynamic and repeated frequency-measurement behaviors. To overcome these limitations, this study integrates multiple cover-pulse modes with reinforcement learning to achieve intelligent and adaptive anti-jamming. Several cover-pulse mechanisms and jammer measurement behaviors are analyzed, and a partially observable Markov decision process is formulated to characterize radar-jammer interactions. A multi-parameter cover-pulse design is introduced to construct a large discrete action space, enabling flexible pulse-frequency and temporal configurations under cognitive jamming. An invalid-action masking mechanism is further incorporated to remove infeasible action combinations and improve decision efficiency in this high-dimensional action space. To address the resulting imperfect-information confrontation problem, a deep Monte Carlo-Enhanced phasic policy gradient algorithm is developed. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed intelligent cover-pulse strategy yields significant performance gains against advanced frequency-measurement jammers. Published: 2026-05-16T06:09:24+00:00 Venue: Signal Processing Score: 0.804 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shanhong Guo; Junxuan Li; Weihao Shi; Bi Wen; Xiaoyu Cong; Weixing Sheng

Journal: Signal Processing

DOI: 10.1016/j.sigpro.2026.110696

Relevance: 0.804 (must_read)

Traditional frequency-agile radars can no longer effectively counter cognitive jammers, and conventional cover-pulse techniques fail to address their dynamic and repeated frequency-measurement behaviors. To overcome these limitations, this study integrates multiple cover-pulse modes with reinforcement learning to achieve intelligent and adaptive anti-jamming. Several cover-pulse mechanisms and jammer measurement behaviors are analyzed, and a partially observable Markov decision process is formulated to characterize radar-jammer interactions. A multi-parameter cover-pulse design is introduced to construct a large discrete action space, enabling flexible pulse-frequency and temporal configurations under cognitive jamming. An invalid-action masking mechanism is further incorporated to remove infeasible action combinations and improve decision efficiency in this high-dimensional action space. To address the resulting imperfect-information confrontation problem, a deep Monte Carlo-Enhanced phasic policy gradient algorithm is developed. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed intelligent cover-pulse strategy yields significant performance gains against advanced frequency-measurement jammers.

item.title: Stochastic Approximation to Contrastive Learning
item.dc:creator: Erland B. Olsson
Zhirong Yang
item.description: Contrastive learning pulls positive samples (similar examples) closer and pushes negative samples (dissimilar examples) away to learn a meaningful mapping from inputs to outputs. It is found in a broad range of applications in computer vision that range from image classification to object detection to video processing and has a similarly broad impact and relevance in natural language processing, audio and speech, graphs, recommendation systems, and multimodal learning. Although contrastive learning done right typically achieves state-of-the-art results, there are major limitations with traditional methods because they rely on arbitrary definitions of positive and negative samples and are not decomposable in minibatch optimization. Thus, they require large batchsizes to effectively manage a tradeoff between positive and negative terms. This approach wastes significant computational resources on negative samples that may have minimal learning signals. To address these limitations, we propose a novel method that reformulates contrastive learning as a matrix approximation problem using I-divergence, a non-normalized variant of Kullback-Leibler divergence. Our objective function is decomposable across instance pairs, enabling efficient stochastic approximation algorithms that perform well with fewer negative samples by leveraging neighbor embeddings. Additionally, we generalize the scaling factor beyond standard normalization to adaptively emphasize positive samples with higher learning potential, reducing computational waste from negative samples. Our ambition is to demonstrate that even with a low batchsize and as few as one negative term per image, our method outperforms existing contrastive learning approaches and is competitive in overall performance to other state-of-the-art approaches on ImageNet which use a higher batchsize. Published: 2026-05-16T15:28:11+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.785 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Erland B. Olsson; Zhirong Yang

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.109098

Relevance: 0.785 (must_read)

Contrastive learning pulls positive samples (similar examples) closer and pushes negative samples (dissimilar examples) away to learn a meaningful mapping from inputs to outputs. It is found in a broad range of applications in computer vision that range from image classification to object detection to video processing and has a similarly broad impact and relevance in natural language processing, audio and speech, graphs, recommendation systems, and multimodal learning. Although contrastive learning done right typically achieves state-of-the-art results, there are major limitations with traditional methods because they rely on arbitrary definitions of positive and negative samples and are not decomposable in minibatch optimization. Thus, they require large batchsizes to effectively manage a tradeoff between positive and negative terms. This approach wastes significant computational resources on negative samples that may have minimal learning signals. To address these limitations, we propose a novel method that reformulates contrastive learning as a matrix approximation problem using I-divergence, a non-normalized variant of Kullback-Leibler divergence. Our objective function is decomposable across instance pairs, enabling efficient stochastic approximation algorithms that perform well with fewer negative samples by leveraging neighbor embeddings. Additionally, we generalize the scaling factor beyond standard normalization to adaptively emphasize positive samples with higher learning potential, reducing computational waste from negative samples. Our ambition is to demonstrate that even with a low batchsize and as few as one negative term per image, our method outperforms existing contrastive learning approaches and is competitive in overall performance to other state-of-the-art approaches on ImageNet which use a higher batchsize.

item.title: Superpixel-informed transformer for multi-label image classification
item.dc:creator: Haixiang Li
Min Fang
Bo Chen
Xiao Li
Guizhi Wang
item.description: Multi-label image classification aims to assign a set of relevant semantic labels to an image. However, current methods often suffer from region evidence confusion, where cues from small objects are diluted by dominant regions. In addition to this visual ambiguity, decoupled modeling remains a common hurdle, as it separates visual feature extraction from label relationship learning. To address these limitations, we propose the Superpixel-informed Transformer (SPiT), a novel end-to-end framework. SPiT adaptively decomposes an image into semantically coherent quasi-object regions using a differentiable superpixel network. Upon these regions, a class-query-based Transformer then performs unified evidence aggregation and label dependency modeling. Experimental results on PASCAL VOC 2007, MS-COCO 2014, and ISIC 2018 demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, achieving mAP scores of 93.06%, 81.73%, and 56.72%, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques on all three benchmarks. Published: 2026-05-16T15:29:08+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Haixiang Li; Min Fang; Bo Chen; Xiao Li; Guizhi Wang

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133995

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Multi-label image classification aims to assign a set of relevant semantic labels to an image. However, current methods often suffer from region evidence confusion, where cues from small objects are diluted by dominant regions. In addition to this visual ambiguity, decoupled modeling remains a common hurdle, as it separates visual feature extraction from label relationship learning. To address these limitations, we propose the Superpixel-informed Transformer (SPiT), a novel end-to-end framework. SPiT adaptively decomposes an image into semantically coherent quasi-object regions using a differentiable superpixel network. Upon these regions, a class-query-based Transformer then performs unified evidence aggregation and label dependency modeling. Experimental results on PASCAL VOC 2007, MS-COCO 2014, and ISIC 2018 demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, achieving mAP scores of 93.06%, 81.73%, and 56.72%, respectively, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques on all three benchmarks.

item.title: Divide and Conquer: Multi-channel Inconsistency Learning Network for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection
item.dc:creator: Lisong Ou
Zhixin Li
item.description: Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) aims to model the inconsistency between text and image. Despite significant progress in this field, existing methods are often limited to focusing on single-granularity inconsistencies, while overlooking the multi-granular semantic contradictions inherent in different irony types. Furthermore, how to flexibly integrate multiple tasks to achieve reliable decision-making remains a critical issue that demands urgent exploration. To address these issues, we propose a novel multi-channel inconsistency learning network, which utilizes a divide-and-conquer strategy to enable refined handling of various sarcasm types within multiple channels. Specifically, we first introduce the cross-modal prior knowledge from CLIP to enrich and guide the learning of unimodal features. Subsequently, we construct multi-granular channels to adapt to different types of sarcastic expressions, thereby comprehensively capturing inconsistency information. Furthermore, we specifically design quality-aware multi-channel fusion method to further enhance the reliability of sarcasm detection results. Extensive experiments and in-depth analyses on publicly available benchmark datasets for multimodal sarcasm detection demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Published: 2026-05-16T00:28:34+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lisong Ou; Zhixin Li

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104479

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Multimodal sarcasm detection (MSD) aims to model the inconsistency between text and image. Despite significant progress in this field, existing methods are often limited to focusing on single-granularity inconsistencies, while overlooking the multi-granular semantic contradictions inherent in different irony types. Furthermore, how to flexibly integrate multiple tasks to achieve reliable decision-making remains a critical issue that demands urgent exploration. To address these issues, we propose a novel multi-channel inconsistency learning network, which utilizes a divide-and-conquer strategy to enable refined handling of various sarcasm types within multiple channels. Specifically, we first introduce the cross-modal prior knowledge from CLIP to enrich and guide the learning of unimodal features. Subsequently, we construct multi-granular channels to adapt to different types of sarcastic expressions, thereby comprehensively capturing inconsistency information. Furthermore, we specifically design quality-aware multi-channel fusion method to further enhance the reliability of sarcasm detection results. Extensive experiments and in-depth analyses on publicly available benchmark datasets for multimodal sarcasm detection demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Knowledge-injected prompt tuning with semantic regularization for fine-grained image recognition
item.dc:creator: Qinyu Zhang
Xinda Liu
Qi Liu
Pengbo Zhou
Guohua Geng
item.description: Fine-grained image recognition requires models to precisely represent subtle but discriminative local semantic attributes that distinguish visually similar categories. Recent advances in vision-language models demonstrate strong semantic representation capabilities, yet their performance heavily relies on the effectiveness of prompt tuning strategies. However, current prompt tuning methods typically represent each category using a single, globally uniform semantic prompt, which inevitably overlooks the diverse and fine-grained attributes essential for accurately distinguishing visually similar categories. To this end, we propose Knowledge-Injected Prompt Tuning with Semantic Regularization (KIP) , a method that injects fine-grained knowledge into prompts to explicitly encode and preserve diverse semantic attributes within the prompt embedding space. Specifically, KIP employs an independent encoding strategy to distinctly represent multiple fine-grained semantic descriptions derived from Large Language Models (LLMs). Moreover, to address the semantic inconsistency caused by hallucinations in LLMs, we introduce a novel semantic regularization mechanism based on Sinkhorn divergence. Extensive experiments across 11 widely used fine-grained image benchmarks demonstrate that KIP significantly enhances performance in cross-dataset transfer, base-to-novel generalization, and few-shot learning tasks. Notably, it achieves substantial improvements in the harmonic mean metric, highlighting its effectiveness and versatility when integrated with existing prompt tuning frameworks. The source code will be released publicly upon acceptance. Published: 2026-05-16T15:30:22+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.778 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qinyu Zhang; Xinda Liu; Qi Liu; Pengbo Zhou; Guohua Geng

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116115

Relevance: 0.778 (consider)

Fine-grained image recognition requires models to precisely represent subtle but discriminative local semantic attributes that distinguish visually similar categories. Recent advances in vision-language models demonstrate strong semantic representation capabilities, yet their performance heavily relies on the effectiveness of prompt tuning strategies. However, current prompt tuning methods typically represent each category using a single, globally uniform semantic prompt, which inevitably overlooks the diverse and fine-grained attributes essential for accurately distinguishing visually similar categories. To this end, we propose Knowledge-Injected Prompt Tuning with Semantic Regularization (KIP) , a method that injects fine-grained knowledge into prompts to explicitly encode and preserve diverse semantic attributes within the prompt embedding space. Specifically, KIP employs an independent encoding strategy to distinctly represent multiple fine-grained semantic descriptions derived from Large Language Models (LLMs). Moreover, to address the semantic inconsistency caused by hallucinations in LLMs, we introduce a novel semantic regularization mechanism based on Sinkhorn divergence. Extensive experiments across 11 widely used fine-grained image benchmarks demonstrate that KIP significantly enhances performance in cross-dataset transfer, base-to-novel generalization, and few-shot learning tasks. Notably, it achieves substantial improvements in the harmonic mean metric, highlighting its effectiveness and versatility when integrated with existing prompt tuning frameworks. The source code will be released publicly upon acceptance.

item.title: DFBSNet: Dual frequency-domain branch fusion and selection network for hyperspectral anomaly detection
item.dc:creator: Yiming Yao
Qing Wang
Dong Zhao
Mingtao You
Pei Xiang
Yuta Asano
Xin Yu
Chao Wang
Huixin Zhou
Jinchang Ren
item.description: Current dual-domain processing methods lack sufficient cross-branch interaction and an adaptive mechanism for fusing and selecting low-frequency and high-frequency representations, resulting in inadequate spatial–spectral exploitation. To address this challenge, we propose a dual frequency-domain branch fusion and selection network (DFBSNet) for hyperspectral anomaly detection. First, we design a multi-scale atrous pooling decomposition module (MSAPD) that fuses paths with small atrous convolutions and large receptive fields to reduce background interference. Second, to suppress the impact of redundant information, we develop a low-frequency processing branch (LFPB) and construct a fusion-based attention selection module (FASM) within it. In addition, to enhance anomalies while avoiding the loss of fine details, we build a high-frequency processing branch (HFPB) with a high-frequency focus enhancement module (HFFEM). Then, the low-frequency and high-frequency features are fused to reconstruct the background. The anomaly detection results are obtained by computing the Mahalanobis distances from the original image features to the reconstructed background and the HFPB-produced anomaly-enhanced features, respectively. The advantage of the proposed method is that it jointly considers background reconstruction quality and anomaly saliency, which significantly improves the accuracy and robustness of anomaly detection. Experimental results on four real-world hyperspectral datasets show that DFBSNet achieves better performance than nine state-of-the-art methods on several evaluation metrics. Published: 2026-05-16T15:21:17+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.775 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yiming Yao; Qing Wang; Dong Zhao; Mingtao You; Pei Xiang; Yuta Asano; Xin Yu; Chao Wang; Huixin Zhou; Jinchang Ren

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113967

Relevance: 0.775 (consider)

Current dual-domain processing methods lack sufficient cross-branch interaction and an adaptive mechanism for fusing and selecting low-frequency and high-frequency representations, resulting in inadequate spatial–spectral exploitation. To address this challenge, we propose a dual frequency-domain branch fusion and selection network (DFBSNet) for hyperspectral anomaly detection. First, we design a multi-scale atrous pooling decomposition module (MSAPD) that fuses paths with small atrous convolutions and large receptive fields to reduce background interference. Second, to suppress the impact of redundant information, we develop a low-frequency processing branch (LFPB) and construct a fusion-based attention selection module (FASM) within it. In addition, to enhance anomalies while avoiding the loss of fine details, we build a high-frequency processing branch (HFPB) with a high-frequency focus enhancement module (HFFEM). Then, the low-frequency and high-frequency features are fused to reconstruct the background. The anomaly detection results are obtained by computing the Mahalanobis distances from the original image features to the reconstructed background and the HFPB-produced anomaly-enhanced features, respectively. The advantage of the proposed method is that it jointly considers background reconstruction quality and anomaly saliency, which significantly improves the accuracy and robustness of anomaly detection. Experimental results on four real-world hyperspectral datasets show that DFBSNet achieves better performance than nine state-of-the-art methods on several evaluation metrics.

item.title: A novel multi-source attention-enhanced transformer U-net for sea surface salinity retrieval based on remote sensing data in the Northwest Pacific
item.dc:creator: Xu Han
Xuefeng Zhang
Dianjun Zhang
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation
item.description: Accurate mapping of sea surface salinity (SSS) is essential for diagnosing the marine hydrological cycle and enhancing coupled atmosphere–ocean forecasts. However, current L-band satellite products remain compromised by radio-frequency interference, land-sea contamination and imperfect roughness corrections. Therefore, this study developed a multi-source attention-enhanced Transformer U-Net (MSAtt-TransUNet), which synergistically exploits Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) L-band brightness temperature (TB) and auxiliary environmental factors, adopting a physically guided loss function that preserves the salinity front to retrieve SSS in the dynamically complex Northwest Pacific. MSAtt-TransUNet reduced Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 12.6% relative to the baseline U-Net. Compared with in situ Argo data, the proposed model attained an RMSE of 0.235 psu and a Pearson correlation coefficient (R) of 0.844, markedly outperforming the operational SMAP Level-3 product (0.338 psu and 0.728). Validation against the BOA_Argo gridded dataset further confirms that MSAtt-TransUNet had better performance than SMAP official, while remaining robust across spatio-temporal domains. Notably, spatial diagnostics against the Hybrid Coordinate Ocean Model (HYCOM) analysis indicate that MSAtt-TransUNet effectively reconstructed salinity gradients across the Northwest Pacific. Shapley additive explanation and channel ablation experiments assigned the highest importance to dual-polarized TB, followed by wind speed and sea surface temperature. The model achieved these gains without assimilating any in situ measurements or model priors, offering a highly accurate, robust, and interpretable alternative for near real-time SSS monitoring and regional data-assimilation applications. Published: 2026-05-17T18:49:24+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation Score: 0.771 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xu Han; Xuefeng Zhang; Dianjun Zhang

Journal: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation

DOI: 10.1016/j.jag.2026.105341

Relevance: 0.771 (consider)

Accurate mapping of sea surface salinity (SSS) is essential for diagnosing the marine hydrological cycle and enhancing coupled atmosphere–ocean forecasts. However, current L-band satellite products remain compromised by radio-frequency interference, land-sea contamination and imperfect roughness corrections. Therefore, this study developed a multi-source attention-enhanced Transformer U-Net (MSAtt-TransUNet), which synergistically exploits Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) L-band brightness temperature (TB) and auxiliary environmental factors, adopting a physically guided loss function that preserves the salinity front to retrieve SSS in the dynamically complex Northwest Pacific. MSAtt-TransUNet reduced Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by 12.6% relative to the baseline U-Net. Compared with in situ Argo data, the proposed model attained an RMSE of 0.235 psu and a Pearson correlation coefficient (R) of 0.844, markedly outperforming the operational SMAP Level-3 product (0.338 psu and 0.728). Validation against the BOA_Argo gridded dataset further confirms that MSAtt-TransUNet had better performance than SMAP official, while remaining robust across spatio-temporal domains. Notably, spatial diagnostics against the Hybrid Coordinate Ocean Model (HYCOM) analysis indicate that MSAtt-TransUNet effectively reconstructed salinity gradients across the Northwest Pacific. Shapley additive explanation and channel ablation experiments assigned the highest importance to dual-polarized TB, followed by wind speed and sea surface temperature. The model achieved these gains without assimilating any in situ measurements or model priors, offering a highly accurate, robust, and interpretable alternative for near real-time SSS monitoring and regional data-assimilation applications.

item.title: CrossPlace: Cross-Modal Place Recognition between Fisheye Cameras and LiDAR via a Unified Descriptor Space
item.dc:creator: Juan José Cabrera
Marcos Alfaro
María Flores
Álvaro Martínez
Arturo Gil
Luis Payá
item.description: This paper presents CrossPlace, an innovative method for cross-modal place recognition between heterogeneous sensor modalities, particularly between fisheye cameras and LiDAR. Place recognition is the fundamental capability of mobile robots to determine their most likely location within a database, based on sensory input queries. In cross-modal place recognition, the goal is to localize using a different sensor from the one originally used to construct the database. The core contribution of this paper is a unified feature space that integrates intensity, depth and semantic information. Both the database entries and the queries are obtained by embedding sensor readings through the same CrossPlace model, ensuring a consistent representation across modalities. Consequently, a database constructed from LiDAR can be queried with fisheye images, and vice versa, using a single shared architecture. Furthermore, a comprehensive data transformation and preprocessing pipeline is presented. Specifically, CrossPlace is constituted by three independent branches, each one for processing intensity, depth and semantic information. Each branch consists of a CosPlace model for image embedding with shared weights across sensor modalities. Late fusion through concatenation of the intensity, depth and semantic embeddings provides optimal global performance. We conduct an exhaustive evaluation on the KITTI-360 dataset, where CrossPlace surpasses state-of-the-art techniques across all metrics, establishing a new standard for cross-modal place recognition in urban and highway environments. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our unified approach for place recognition across different sensor modalities while maintaining a robust performance under various operating environments. Published: 2026-05-16T00:02:41+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.770 (consider)

Authors: Juan José Cabrera; Marcos Alfaro; María Flores; Álvaro Martínez; Arturo Gil; Luis Payá

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132838

Relevance: 0.770 (consider)

This paper presents CrossPlace, an innovative method for cross-modal place recognition between heterogeneous sensor modalities, particularly between fisheye cameras and LiDAR. Place recognition is the fundamental capability of mobile robots to determine their most likely location within a database, based on sensory input queries. In cross-modal place recognition, the goal is to localize using a different sensor from the one originally used to construct the database. The core contribution of this paper is a unified feature space that integrates intensity, depth and semantic information. Both the database entries and the queries are obtained by embedding sensor readings through the same CrossPlace model, ensuring a consistent representation across modalities. Consequently, a database constructed from LiDAR can be queried with fisheye images, and vice versa, using a single shared architecture. Furthermore, a comprehensive data transformation and preprocessing pipeline is presented. Specifically, CrossPlace is constituted by three independent branches, each one for processing intensity, depth and semantic information. Each branch consists of a CosPlace model for image embedding with shared weights across sensor modalities. Late fusion through concatenation of the intensity, depth and semantic embeddings provides optimal global performance. We conduct an exhaustive evaluation on the KITTI-360 dataset, where CrossPlace surpasses state-of-the-art techniques across all metrics, establishing a new standard for cross-modal place recognition in urban and highway environments. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our unified approach for place recognition across different sensor modalities while maintaining a robust performance under various operating environments.

item.title: Distinguishing visually similar actions: Prompt-guided semantic prototype modulation for few-shot action recognition
item.dc:creator: Xiaoyang Li
Mingming Lu
Ruiqi Wang
Hao Li
Zewei Le
item.description: Few-shot action recognition aims to enable models to quickly learn new action categories from a limited number of labeled samples, addressing the challenge of data scarcity in real-world applications. Current research primarily faces three core challenges: (1) temporal modeling, where models are prone to interference from irrelevant static background information and struggle to capture the essence of dynamic action features; (2) visual similarity, where categories with subtle visual differences are difficult to distinguish; and (3) the modality gap between visual-textual support prototypes and visual-only queries, thereby complicating alignment within a shared embedding space. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a CLIP-SPM framework, which includes three components: (1) the Hierarchical Synergistic Motion Regularization (HSMR) module, which aligns deep and shallow motion features to improve temporal modeling by reducing static background interference; (2) the Semantic Prototype Modulation (SPM) strategy, which generates query-relevant text prompts to bridge the modality gap and integrates them with visual features, enhancing the discriminability between similar actions; and (3) the Prototype-Anchor Dual Modulation (PADM) method, which refines support prototypes and aligns query features with a global semantic anchor, improving consistency across support and query samples. Comprehensive experiments across standard benchmarks, including Kinetics, SSv2-Full, SSv2-Small, UCF101, and HMDB51, demonstrate that our CLIP-SPM achieves competitive performance under 1-shot, 3-shot, and 5-shot settings. Extensive ablation studies and visual analyses further validate the effectiveness of each component and its contributions to addressing the core challenges.The source code and models are publicly available at GitHub . Published: 2026-05-16T00:02:15+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.769 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaoyang Li; Mingming Lu; Ruiqi Wang; Hao Li; Zewei Le

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116181

Relevance: 0.769 (consider)

Few-shot action recognition aims to enable models to quickly learn new action categories from a limited number of labeled samples, addressing the challenge of data scarcity in real-world applications. Current research primarily faces three core challenges: (1) temporal modeling, where models are prone to interference from irrelevant static background information and struggle to capture the essence of dynamic action features; (2) visual similarity, where categories with subtle visual differences are difficult to distinguish; and (3) the modality gap between visual-textual support prototypes and visual-only queries, thereby complicating alignment within a shared embedding space. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a CLIP-SPM framework, which includes three components: (1) the Hierarchical Synergistic Motion Regularization (HSMR) module, which aligns deep and shallow motion features to improve temporal modeling by reducing static background interference; (2) the Semantic Prototype Modulation (SPM) strategy, which generates query-relevant text prompts to bridge the modality gap and integrates them with visual features, enhancing the discriminability between similar actions; and (3) the Prototype-Anchor Dual Modulation (PADM) method, which refines support prototypes and aligns query features with a global semantic anchor, improving consistency across support and query samples. Comprehensive experiments across standard benchmarks, including Kinetics, SSv2-Full, SSv2-Small, UCF101, and HMDB51, demonstrate that our CLIP-SPM achieves competitive performance under 1-shot, 3-shot, and 5-shot settings. Extensive ablation studies and visual analyses further validate the effectiveness of each component and its contributions to addressing the core challenges.The source code and models are publicly available at GitHub .

item.title: National-scale mapping of staple crops in Nepal with multi-year Sentinel-2 L2A imagery and binary transformer models
item.dc:creator: Dong Luo
Kunwar K. Singh
Seth Goodman
Ariel BenYishay
item.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation
item.description: Accurate mapping of staple crops using satellite imagery is essential for sustainable agricultural management, particularly in data-limited regions. Despite recent advances in Earth observations and deep learning, using multi-year time series satellite data and binary deep learning models to produce national-scale multi-class crop type map remains underexplored. In this study, we attempt to map staple crops, other crops, and non-crop at 10 m resolution that leverages multi-year, unsmoothed Sentinel-2A/B MSI Level-2A time series imagery, binary transformer models, and a probability-based thresholding method. Nepal was selected as the case study due to its complex topography and heterogeneous landscapes. We developed six experimental setups, and models achieved an average overall accuracy of 91.39%, with class-wise F1-scores ranging from 0.81 to 0.96. The best-performing model was then applied to 28 tiles covering Nepal, and a probability-based thresholding approach was used to assemble binary results into a multi-class map. The map overall accuracy was higher than 94%, when validated with field data. Sensitivity analyses further confirmed the robustness and spatial consistency of the proposed framework across diverse land-cover and agroecological zones. Finally, the produced national-scale maps indicated 3.91% increase in staple crops areas from 2021 to 2023 in Nepal. Published: 2026-05-16T12:16:19+00:00 Venue: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation Score: 0.768 (consider)

Journal: International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation

Relevance: 0.768 (consider)

Accurate mapping of staple crops using satellite imagery is essential for sustainable agricultural management, particularly in data-limited regions. Despite recent advances in Earth observations and deep learning, using multi-year time series satellite data and binary deep learning models to produce national-scale multi-class crop type map remains underexplored. In this study, we attempt to map staple crops, other crops, and non-crop at 10 m resolution that leverages multi-year, unsmoothed Sentinel-2A/B MSI Level-2A time series imagery, binary transformer models, and a probability-based thresholding method. Nepal was selected as the case study due to its complex topography and heterogeneous landscapes. We developed six experimental setups, and models achieved an average overall accuracy of 91.39%, with class-wise F1-scores ranging from 0.81 to 0.96. The best-performing model was then applied to 28 tiles covering Nepal, and a probability-based thresholding approach was used to assemble binary results into a multi-class map. The map overall accuracy was higher than 94%, when validated with field data. Sensitivity analyses further confirmed the robustness and spatial consistency of the proposed framework across diverse land-cover and agroecological zones. Finally, the produced national-scale maps indicated 3.91% increase in staple crops areas from 2021 to 2023 in Nepal.

item.title: T-ACD: A topography-aware change detection method for mapping high-resolution soil moisture with time-series SAR
item.dc:creator: Haoyu Pei
Hao Sun
Yunjia Wang
item.description: High-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has emerged as a promising tool for regional-scale soil moisture monitoring. However, most change detection–based retrieval methods implicitly assume a planar surface and represent both surface scattering and vegetation attenuation using a single nominal incidence angle. Under complex terrain or vegetated conditions, deviations in the local incidence angle are nonlinearly amplified through nonlinear scattering and transmission processes, leading to severe radiometric distortion and degraded retrieval accuracy. To address this issue, this study investigates the physical role of local incidence angle deviations in change detection–based soil moisture retrieval and proposes a topography-aware change detection (T-ACD) method. By explicitly incorporating the local incidence angle into the retrieval formulation, T-ACD provides a geometrically consistent description of both surface scattering and vegetation attenuation. Multi-scale validation was conducted over two representative areas in China. In the topographically complex Wudaoliang area, T-ACD effectively suppresses geometry-induced error amplification and outperforms conventional advanced change detection (ACD) and short-term change detection (STCD) methods. In the relatively flat but densely vegetated Shandian River area, point-scale validation against in situ stations yields an average correlation (R) of 0.53, an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.067 m 3 /m 3 , and an average unbiased RMSE (ubRMSE) of 0.051 m 3 /m 3 across all sites. Furthermore, regional-scale evaluation against airborne reference data demonstrates enhanced spatial representation, achieving a pixel-wise correlation of R ≈ 0.50, alongside a root mean square difference (RMSD) and an unbiased RMSD (ubRMSD) of approximately 0.060 and 0.028 m 3 /m 3 , respectively. Overall, these results demonstrate that explicitly accounting for local incidence angle effects is essential in both rugged and ostensibly flat landscapes, enhancing the physical consistency and robustness of high-resolution SAR soil moisture retrieval. Published: 2026-05-16T06:40:02+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.767 (consider)

Authors: Haoyu Pei; Hao Sun; Yunjia Wang

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115486

Relevance: 0.767 (consider)

High-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) has emerged as a promising tool for regional-scale soil moisture monitoring. However, most change detection–based retrieval methods implicitly assume a planar surface and represent both surface scattering and vegetation attenuation using a single nominal incidence angle. Under complex terrain or vegetated conditions, deviations in the local incidence angle are nonlinearly amplified through nonlinear scattering and transmission processes, leading to severe radiometric distortion and degraded retrieval accuracy. To address this issue, this study investigates the physical role of local incidence angle deviations in change detection–based soil moisture retrieval and proposes a topography-aware change detection (T-ACD) method. By explicitly incorporating the local incidence angle into the retrieval formulation, T-ACD provides a geometrically consistent description of both surface scattering and vegetation attenuation. Multi-scale validation was conducted over two representative areas in China. In the topographically complex Wudaoliang area, T-ACD effectively suppresses geometry-induced error amplification and outperforms conventional advanced change detection (ACD) and short-term change detection (STCD) methods. In the relatively flat but densely vegetated Shandian River area, point-scale validation against in situ stations yields an average correlation (R) of 0.53, an average root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.067 m 3 /m 3 , and an average unbiased RMSE (ubRMSE) of 0.051 m 3 /m 3 across all sites. Furthermore, regional-scale evaluation against airborne reference data demonstrates enhanced spatial representation, achieving a pixel-wise correlation of R ≈ 0.50, alongside a root mean square difference (RMSD) and an unbiased RMSD (ubRMSD) of approximately 0.060 and 0.028 m 3 /m 3 , respectively. Overall, these results demonstrate that explicitly accounting for local incidence angle effects is essential in both rugged and ostensibly flat landscapes, enhancing the physical consistency and robustness of high-resolution SAR soil moisture retrieval.

item.title: MGCFI-Net: Multi-Scale Globally Aware Feature Learning with Cross-View Feature Interaction for Multi-View Stereo
item.dc:creator: Ming Han
Hui Yin
Aixin Chong
Hua Huang
item.description: Recent learning-based multi-view stereo (MVS) methods have achieved notable progress, yet accurate and complete reconstruction in weakly textured regions remains challenging. To address this issue, we present MGCFI-Net (Multi-Scale Globally Aware Cross-View Feature Interaction Network), which is able to learn multi-scale globally aware representations and explicitly model cross-view feature interactions under supervision. Specifically, MGCFI-Net incorporates a Multi-Scale Hierarchical Perception (MSHP) module that integrates the standard convolution, parallel dilated convolutions, and a Swin Transformer to progressively capture discriminative features with global contextual awareness. For multi-scale feature fusion, we use patch-expanding upsampling to preserve semantics, improving cross-scale integration. To enhance cross-view geometric consistency, a Cross-View Feature Interaction (CVFI) module is introduced, in which epipolar self-attention is applied within each source feature and epipolar cross-attention is performed between each source feature and the reference feature, enabling more reliable reference–source feature matching. Furthermore, we incorporate Cross-View Feature Similarity Supervision (CFSS), which enforces feature-level consistency through geometric warping and improves cross-view alignment and matching robustness under challenging conditions. Extensive evaluations on the DTU, Tanks & Temples, and ETH3D benchmarks demonstrate that MGCFI-Net achieves superior reconstruction quality and establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability. Published: 2026-05-16T15:28:28+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ming Han; Hui Yin; Aixin Chong; Hua Huang

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.109137

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Recent learning-based multi-view stereo (MVS) methods have achieved notable progress, yet accurate and complete reconstruction in weakly textured regions remains challenging. To address this issue, we present MGCFI-Net (Multi-Scale Globally Aware Cross-View Feature Interaction Network), which is able to learn multi-scale globally aware representations and explicitly model cross-view feature interactions under supervision. Specifically, MGCFI-Net incorporates a Multi-Scale Hierarchical Perception (MSHP) module that integrates the standard convolution, parallel dilated convolutions, and a Swin Transformer to progressively capture discriminative features with global contextual awareness. For multi-scale feature fusion, we use patch-expanding upsampling to preserve semantics, improving cross-scale integration. To enhance cross-view geometric consistency, a Cross-View Feature Interaction (CVFI) module is introduced, in which epipolar self-attention is applied within each source feature and epipolar cross-attention is performed between each source feature and the reference feature, enabling more reliable reference–source feature matching. Furthermore, we incorporate Cross-View Feature Similarity Supervision (CFSS), which enforces feature-level consistency through geometric warping and improves cross-view alignment and matching robustness under challenging conditions. Extensive evaluations on the DTU, Tanks & Temples, and ETH3D benchmarks demonstrate that MGCFI-Net achieves superior reconstruction quality and establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability.

item.title: Unmasking the Tiny: Foreground probing for small object detection
item.dc:creator: Tong Zhang
Hainuo Wang
Yi Zhang
Xiaojie Guo
item.description: Detecting small objects in high-resolution images is challenging, as small targets are often overwhelmed by the surrounding background and thus prone to being missed or misclassified. To address this issue, this work proposes a foreground probing paradigm to recover and refine suppressed foreground scores by leveraging collective classification features. At its core, we introduce a sparse token selection module (STSM) that identifies potential foreground tokens across feature maps, for the sake of preserving promising candidates from being submerged within background features. To further enhance these representations, we design a foreground refinement module (FRM) that distills a semantically enriched attention map from classification features to guide information aggregation. This allows tokens to adaptively reference semantically similar neighbors, thereby strengthening discrimination between foreground and background in complex scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a superior performance on small object detection. Our code is available at: https://github.com/AngstCJ/Unmasking-the-Tiny-Foreground-Probing-for-Small-Object-Detection . Published: 2026-05-16T15:25:05+00:00 Venue: Image and Vision Computing Score: 0.765 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tong Zhang; Hainuo Wang; Yi Zhang; Xiaojie Guo

Journal: Image and Vision Computing

DOI: 10.1016/j.imavis.2026.106026

Relevance: 0.765 (consider)

Detecting small objects in high-resolution images is challenging, as small targets are often overwhelmed by the surrounding background and thus prone to being missed or misclassified. To address this issue, this work proposes a foreground probing paradigm to recover and refine suppressed foreground scores by leveraging collective classification features. At its core, we introduce a sparse token selection module (STSM) that identifies potential foreground tokens across feature maps, for the sake of preserving promising candidates from being submerged within background features. To further enhance these representations, we design a foreground refinement module (FRM) that distills a semantically enriched attention map from classification features to guide information aggregation. This allows tokens to adaptively reference semantically similar neighbors, thereby strengthening discrimination between foreground and background in complex scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a superior performance on small object detection. Our code is available at: https://github.com/AngstCJ/Unmasking-the-Tiny-Foreground-Probing-for-Small-Object-Detection .

item.title: A multi-view feature collaborative optimization method for object detection
item.dc:creator: Jiaji Liu
Runhai Jiao
Xiangning Zhan
Kaihang Li
item.description: In current multi-view object detection research, the acquisition of authentic multi-view data typically demands expensive hardware. Moreover, existing fusion methods struggle to ensure consistency and complementarity across views, severely limiting detection performance. To address these challenges, a method combing multi-view data generation with feature collaborative optimization to enhance single-view object detection is developed. Specifically, an augmentation-based generation strategy is proposed to synthesize diverse pseudo-multi-view inputs from given single-view RGB images, enabling multi-view mechanism training. Furthermore, a global-local collaborative cosine loss is designed to hierarchically learn global consistency and local complementary information across views, thus producing more discriminative object feature representations. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on three benchmark datasets for both power defect detection and general object detection. Average mAP improvements of about 2.7% and 7.0% are achieved over the corresponding baselines in YOLO-based and Transformer-based detectors, respectively. In addition, a real-time inference speed of nearly 213 FPS is attained on standard hardware, highlighting a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/LiuJiaji1999/MFCO . Published: 2026-05-16T15:24:59+00:00 Venue: Image and Vision Computing Score: 0.764 (consider)

Journal: Image and Vision Computing

DOI: 10.1016/j.imavis.2026.106023

Relevance: 0.764 (consider)

In current multi-view object detection research, the acquisition of authentic multi-view data typically demands expensive hardware. Moreover, existing fusion methods struggle to ensure consistency and complementarity across views, severely limiting detection performance. To address these challenges, a method combing multi-view data generation with feature collaborative optimization to enhance single-view object detection is developed. Specifically, an augmentation-based generation strategy is proposed to synthesize diverse pseudo-multi-view inputs from given single-view RGB images, enabling multi-view mechanism training. Furthermore, a global-local collaborative cosine loss is designed to hierarchically learn global consistency and local complementary information across views, thus producing more discriminative object feature representations. Experimental results indicate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on three benchmark datasets for both power defect detection and general object detection. Average mAP improvements of about 2.7% and 7.0% are achieved over the corresponding baselines in YOLO-based and Transformer-based detectors, respectively. In addition, a real-time inference speed of nearly 213 FPS is attained on standard hardware, highlighting a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/LiuJiaji1999/MFCO .

item.title: CC-GFRT: Class Correlation-based Granular Feature Refinement and Transfer for Non-Exemplar Class Incremental Learning
item.dc:creator: Ruixuan Gao
Zongyong Deng
Yue Yang
Qijun Zhao
item.description: Non-Exemplar Class Incremental Learning (NECIL) aims to learn a robust model across a sequence of tasks without access to previous task samples. Current methods typically use prototype augmentation and knowledge distillation to mitigate catastrophic forgetting in NECIL. However, two limitations persist. First, their augmented historical prototypes may face difficulty in balancing the retention of old-class discriminability and adaptation to the new feature space, which can lead to blurred decision boundaries. Second, their unselective distillation tends to introduce redundancy in old knowledge and overpenalize new knowledge learning, thereby limiting the plasticity of the feature extractor. To address these issues, we propose a novel Class Correlation-based Granular Feature Refinement and Transfer (CC-GFRT) strategy that leverages the new-old class correlation for granular prototype augmentation and knowledge transfer. Specifically, the Distribution-aware Correlation Measurement (DCM) module first captures new-old class correlation via distribution discrepancies. Then, the Dimension-level Prototype Refinement (DPR) module adapts prototypes to the new feature space by this correlation and performs differentiated dimensional operation to preserve old-class discriminability. Moreover, the Long-term Selective Knowledge Transfer (LSKT) mechanism leverages correlation for class-aware selective distillation, coupled with feature-level adaptive distillation, achieving targeted old knowledge transfer without impeding new knowledge learning. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our method. Compared with SOTA methods, it achieves average accuracy gains of 2.29%, 1.69%, 3.32% on CIFAR-100, 2.17%, 1.68%, 1.70% on TinyImageNet, and 0.05% on ImageNet-subset across various settings. Furthermore, our method achieves competitive performance in the practical Lifelong Person Re-Identification scenario. Published: 2026-05-17T14:47:05+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.759 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Ruixuan Gao; Zongyong Deng; Yue Yang; Qijun Zhao

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.116166

Relevance: 0.759 (consider)

Non-Exemplar Class Incremental Learning (NECIL) aims to learn a robust model across a sequence of tasks without access to previous task samples. Current methods typically use prototype augmentation and knowledge distillation to mitigate catastrophic forgetting in NECIL. However, two limitations persist. First, their augmented historical prototypes may face difficulty in balancing the retention of old-class discriminability and adaptation to the new feature space, which can lead to blurred decision boundaries. Second, their unselective distillation tends to introduce redundancy in old knowledge and overpenalize new knowledge learning, thereby limiting the plasticity of the feature extractor. To address these issues, we propose a novel Class Correlation-based Granular Feature Refinement and Transfer (CC-GFRT) strategy that leverages the new-old class correlation for granular prototype augmentation and knowledge transfer. Specifically, the Distribution-aware Correlation Measurement (DCM) module first captures new-old class correlation via distribution discrepancies. Then, the Dimension-level Prototype Refinement (DPR) module adapts prototypes to the new feature space by this correlation and performs differentiated dimensional operation to preserve old-class discriminability. Moreover, the Long-term Selective Knowledge Transfer (LSKT) mechanism leverages correlation for class-aware selective distillation, coupled with feature-level adaptive distillation, achieving targeted old knowledge transfer without impeding new knowledge learning. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our method. Compared with SOTA methods, it achieves average accuracy gains of 2.29%, 1.69%, 3.32% on CIFAR-100, 2.17%, 1.68%, 1.70% on TinyImageNet, and 0.05% on ImageNet-subset across various settings. Furthermore, our method achieves competitive performance in the practical Lifelong Person Re-Identification scenario.

item.title: Self-orthorectification of satellite images using multi-view geometric constraints without external geospatial data
item.dc:creator: Seunghwan Ban
Taejung Kim
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: Traditional orthorectification methods rely on ground control points (GCPs) and external digital elevation models (DEMs), which may be unavailable or costly to obtain. This study presents a self-orthorectification framework for high-resolution satellite imagery that eliminates the need for such external references by using only overlapping images and their rational polynomial coefficients (RPCs). The proposed method extracts tie points under RPC-based geometric constraints, performs multi-view bundle adjustment to jointly refine relative image orientation and reconstruct 3D ground coordinates, and generates a virtual terrain model for orthorectification. Experiments on two KOMPSAT-3A datasets covering urban and mountainous regions showed that the proposed method reduced the 2D check-point MAE from 8.391 to 0.355 pixels in Seoul and from 2.335 to 0.454 pixels in Jeju, while the corresponding RMSE decreased from 10.151 to 0.512 pixels and from 3.117 to 0.686 pixels, respectively. Compared with existing GCP-free image registration methods that mainly improve relative alignment and DEM-assisted orthorectification approaches that still depend on external elevation data, the proposed framework enables internally consistent orthorectification using only the input image block. The reported accuracy reflects internal geometric consistency among overlapping images rather than absolute planimetric geolocation accuracy. Published: 2026-05-17T16:15:28+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.759 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Seunghwan Ban; Taejung Kim

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.05.015

Relevance: 0.759 (consider)

Traditional orthorectification methods rely on ground control points (GCPs) and external digital elevation models (DEMs), which may be unavailable or costly to obtain. This study presents a self-orthorectification framework for high-resolution satellite imagery that eliminates the need for such external references by using only overlapping images and their rational polynomial coefficients (RPCs). The proposed method extracts tie points under RPC-based geometric constraints, performs multi-view bundle adjustment to jointly refine relative image orientation and reconstruct 3D ground coordinates, and generates a virtual terrain model for orthorectification. Experiments on two KOMPSAT-3A datasets covering urban and mountainous regions showed that the proposed method reduced the 2D check-point MAE from 8.391 to 0.355 pixels in Seoul and from 2.335 to 0.454 pixels in Jeju, while the corresponding RMSE decreased from 10.151 to 0.512 pixels and from 3.117 to 0.686 pixels, respectively. Compared with existing GCP-free image registration methods that mainly improve relative alignment and DEM-assisted orthorectification approaches that still depend on external elevation data, the proposed framework enables internally consistent orthorectification using only the input image block. The reported accuracy reflects internal geometric consistency among overlapping images rather than absolute planimetric geolocation accuracy.

item.title: Integrating Retinex Theory with Atmospheric Scattering Model for Mixed-Degradation Image Recovery
item.dc:creator: Yonglong Jiang
Bo Jiang
Jiahe Zhu
Zelong Tan
Zehua Ji
Hongbing Ma
item.description: Images captured in low-light, hazy environments suffer from mixed degradation: insufficient illumination compresses the dynamic range, while atmospheric scattering obscures textural details. Existing methods, typically designed for single degradation factors, struggle to address these coupled effects. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unified physics-based model that integrates the atmospheric scattering equation with Retinex theory. Based on this model, we introduce the Mixed-Degradation Image Recovery (MDIR) framework, an end-to-end network specifically engineered for concurrent low-light enhancement and dehazing. To support data-driven training, we construct the Low-Light & Foggy Dataset (LFD), a novel collection of 1,900 paired real-world images. Furthermore, MDIR incorporates a superpixel-guided, windowed self-attention mechanism to estimate depth-dependent transmittance, enabling precise, non-uniform haze removal. Extensive experiments on the LFD dataset demonstrate that MDIR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art techniques, achieving average gains of 7.61 dB in PSNR and 0.38 in SSIM. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/MDIR-LFD/MDIR . Published: 2026-05-16T00:26:40+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.757 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yonglong Jiang; Bo Jiang; Jiahe Zhu; Zelong Tan; Zehua Ji; Hongbing Ma

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132543

Relevance: 0.757 (consider)

Images captured in low-light, hazy environments suffer from mixed degradation: insufficient illumination compresses the dynamic range, while atmospheric scattering obscures textural details. Existing methods, typically designed for single degradation factors, struggle to address these coupled effects. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unified physics-based model that integrates the atmospheric scattering equation with Retinex theory. Based on this model, we introduce the Mixed-Degradation Image Recovery (MDIR) framework, an end-to-end network specifically engineered for concurrent low-light enhancement and dehazing. To support data-driven training, we construct the Low-Light & Foggy Dataset (LFD), a novel collection of 1,900 paired real-world images. Furthermore, MDIR incorporates a superpixel-guided, windowed self-attention mechanism to estimate depth-dependent transmittance, enabling precise, non-uniform haze removal. Extensive experiments on the LFD dataset demonstrate that MDIR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art techniques, achieving average gains of 7.61 dB in PSNR and 0.38 in SSIM. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/MDIR-LFD/MDIR .

item.title: FSC-MAE: Feature structure coordinated mask autoencoder
item.dc:creator: Lei Song
Enguang Zuo
Chen Chen
Cheng Chen
Xiaoyi Lv
Ruishuang Sun
Yinhong Li
Hongbing Ma
item.description: Masked graph autoencoders (MAEs) have emerged as a novel self-supervised learning paradigm,which learn node representations by reconstructing masked graph information. Node features and structural features encode semantic information at different levels, making feature fusion and collaborative learning critical for building effective representations. However,most existing methods adopt a single-view masking strategy, masking either node features or graph topology, which fails to fully exploit the synergistic relationship between node attributes and structural information. Moreover, current approaches lack effective modeling of deep contextual information for nodes. To address these limitations, we propose Feature-Structure Collaborative Masked Graph Autoencoder (FSC-MAE). FSC-MAE employs a collaborative masking strategy that simultaneously masks node features and edge connections, forming a self-supervised learning framework with joint feature–structure information loss. Furthermore, we design a feature–structure mutual compensation mechanism to enable bidirectional complementary learning and capture deep semantic relationships from multi-hop neighborhoods, effectively overcoming the shortcomings of traditional methods in collaborative modeling and context awareness.Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that FSC-MAE achieves significant performance improvements on both link prediction and node classification tasks, validating the effectiveness of the proposed collaborative masking strategy. Published: 2026-05-16T00:26:01+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.753 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lei Song; Enguang Zuo; Chen Chen; Cheng Chen; Xiaoyi Lv; Ruishuang Sun; Yinhong Li; Hongbing Ma

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133957

Relevance: 0.753 (consider)

Masked graph autoencoders (MAEs) have emerged as a novel self-supervised learning paradigm,which learn node representations by reconstructing masked graph information. Node features and structural features encode semantic information at different levels, making feature fusion and collaborative learning critical for building effective representations. However,most existing methods adopt a single-view masking strategy, masking either node features or graph topology, which fails to fully exploit the synergistic relationship between node attributes and structural information. Moreover, current approaches lack effective modeling of deep contextual information for nodes. To address these limitations, we propose Feature-Structure Collaborative Masked Graph Autoencoder (FSC-MAE). FSC-MAE employs a collaborative masking strategy that simultaneously masks node features and edge connections, forming a self-supervised learning framework with joint feature–structure information loss. Furthermore, we design a feature–structure mutual compensation mechanism to enable bidirectional complementary learning and capture deep semantic relationships from multi-hop neighborhoods, effectively overcoming the shortcomings of traditional methods in collaborative modeling and context awareness.Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that FSC-MAE achieves significant performance improvements on both link prediction and node classification tasks, validating the effectiveness of the proposed collaborative masking strategy.

item.title: CMGR: Cross-modal graph routing for knowledge-based visual question answering
item.dc:creator: Wei Li
Zhixin Li
item.description: Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to integrate visual content, linguistic queries, and external knowledge for accurate reasoning. Existing methods often suffer from insufficient cross-modal alignment, limited interpretability, and poor performance on zero-shot questions. To address these issues, we propose a Cross-Modal Graph Routing (CMGR) framework, which explicitly conducts step-by-step reasoning over a constructed cross-modal graph. Specifically, CMGR consists of three core components: (1) Scene Knowledge Graph Construction (SKGC) that fuses image scene graphs with external knowledge triples, filtered via cross-modal semantic similarity; (2) Dynamic Graph Routing (DGR) that leverages vision-language joint embeddings to guide entity transition and score propagation, ensuring fine-grained alignment between modalities; (3) Adaptive Answer Prediction (AAP) that unifies entity and relation scoring to handle both seen and unseen answers. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that CMGR outperforms state-of-the-art methods. CMGR exhibits strong interpretability through transparent reasoning paths. Published: 2026-05-17T14:46:13+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.752 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wei Li; Zhixin Li

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.134002

Relevance: 0.752 (consider)

Knowledge-Based Visual Question Answering (KB-VQA) requires models to integrate visual content, linguistic queries, and external knowledge for accurate reasoning. Existing methods often suffer from insufficient cross-modal alignment, limited interpretability, and poor performance on zero-shot questions. To address these issues, we propose a Cross-Modal Graph Routing (CMGR) framework, which explicitly conducts step-by-step reasoning over a constructed cross-modal graph. Specifically, CMGR consists of three core components: (1) Scene Knowledge Graph Construction (SKGC) that fuses image scene graphs with external knowledge triples, filtered via cross-modal semantic similarity; (2) Dynamic Graph Routing (DGR) that leverages vision-language joint embeddings to guide entity transition and score propagation, ensuring fine-grained alignment between modalities; (3) Adaptive Answer Prediction (AAP) that unifies entity and relation scoring to handle both seen and unseen answers. Experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that CMGR outperforms state-of-the-art methods. CMGR exhibits strong interpretability through transparent reasoning paths.

item.title: AND-GS: Adaptive supervision of normal and depth in Gaussian splatting for accurate and efficient surface reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Xiang Le
Qiang Zhao
Haofan Ren
Zhongtian Zheng
Tingyu Wang
Jiyong Zhang
Chenggang Yan
item.description: Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated stunning results in novel view synthesis, enabling real-time rendering of fine-grained images. Nevertheless, using 3D Gaussians for surface reconstruction presents prominent challenges. Previous methods either introduce the monocular predicted 3D scene cues, which usually lose the fine geometric details, or exploit the inherent constraints between depths and normals, which may fall into local minima and lead to low convergence speed. In this study, we present a new framework AND-GS that adaptively supervises the rendered depth and normal in the 3DGS optimization procedure for accurate and efficient surface reconstruction. Our method takes advantage of both the monocular predicted 3D scene cues and the inherent constraints between depths and normals, and proposes a new training strategy that adaptively switches between these two constraints according to the similarity between low-frequency information of the rendered images and the input image. This strategy can significantly improve the convergence speed and the final reconstruction quality. Our evaluations demonstrate that AND-GS achieves superior performance compared to existing 3DGS-based methods in both surface reconstruction and novel view synthesis. Furthermore, it delivers comparable or even better results than neural implicit methods, excelling in both quality and computational efficiency. We plan to release our code as open source upon acceptance of this paper. Published: 2026-05-16T15:29:00+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.752 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiang Le; Qiang Zhao; Haofan Ren; Zhongtian Zheng; Tingyu Wang; Jiyong Zhang; Chenggang Yan

Journal: Neurocomputing

DOI: 10.1016/j.neucom.2026.133980

Relevance: 0.752 (consider)

Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has demonstrated stunning results in novel view synthesis, enabling real-time rendering of fine-grained images. Nevertheless, using 3D Gaussians for surface reconstruction presents prominent challenges. Previous methods either introduce the monocular predicted 3D scene cues, which usually lose the fine geometric details, or exploit the inherent constraints between depths and normals, which may fall into local minima and lead to low convergence speed. In this study, we present a new framework AND-GS that adaptively supervises the rendered depth and normal in the 3DGS optimization procedure for accurate and efficient surface reconstruction. Our method takes advantage of both the monocular predicted 3D scene cues and the inherent constraints between depths and normals, and proposes a new training strategy that adaptively switches between these two constraints according to the similarity between low-frequency information of the rendered images and the input image. This strategy can significantly improve the convergence speed and the final reconstruction quality. Our evaluations demonstrate that AND-GS achieves superior performance compared to existing 3DGS-based methods in both surface reconstruction and novel view synthesis. Furthermore, it delivers comparable or even better results than neural implicit methods, excelling in both quality and computational efficiency. We plan to release our code as open source upon acceptance of this paper.

item.title: DyLite-VSR: A dynamic and lightweight video super-resolution network for highly compressed videos
item.dc:creator: Ilhwan Kwon
Jun Li
Mahardhika Pratama
Mukesh Prasad
item.description: Services based on various forms of video streaming deliver content to clients by compressing originally captured videos using video codecs. During this process, due to limitations such as network bandwidth and display device capabilities, the original resolution is often reduced or a higher compression rate is applied, resulting in smaller data sizes being transmitted. Consequently, the video quality experienced by end users is often degraded. To address this issue, numerous AI-based Video Super-Resolution (VSR) techniques have been proposed. However, in addition to their high architectural complexity, many of these models require a large number of input frames or rely on recurrent frameworks, which further complicate both training and inference. These factors present significant challenges for deployment in real-time video services. In this paper, we propose a lightweight VSR model that achieves high performance even on low-quality, highly compressed video content. We propose an adaptive inference method that dynamically selects between lightweight and enhanced processing modules based on the input video’s resolution, thereby improving the suitability of our approach for real-time video streaming applications. Additionally, we visualize the performance of the proposed model with Grad-CAM to demonstrate its effectiveness compared to existing methods. Published: 2026-05-16T15:30:11+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.750 (consider)
